--- SOURCE 5: [PDF] Roles for City Governments in Entrepreneurial Ecosystems - RAND ---
URL: https://www.rand.org/content/dam/rand/pubs/research_reports/RRA1100/RRA1141-1/RAND_RRA1141-1.pdf

SUMMARY:
Local policymakers are looking to support entrepreneurial ecosystems as a way to promote economic development and pathways to the middle class more generally,

FULL CONTENT:
ELSIE HARPER-ANDERSON, KELSEY O’HOLLAREN, SUSAN M. GATES Roles for City Governments in Entrepreneurial Ecosystems An Initial Exploration O n the heels of the coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic, the U.S. economy expe-rienced an entrepreneurial renaissance. New business applications increased dramatically in the second half of 2020 to levels never observed in the data and have remained substan-tially above pre-pandemic levels through September 2022 (Haltiwanger, 2021; U.S. Census Bureau, 2022). National data on the number of active small businesses and small business revenue tell a similar story (Chetty et al., 2022), showing an increase of 3.1 percent in the number of such businesses and an 8 percent increase in their revenue between January 2020 and February 2022 despite sharp declines in both indicators in the early months of the COVID-19 pandemic.1 Available data suggest that national trends hide substantial local varia-tion. Some metropolitan areas, such as Detroit, experienced dramatic growth in both indica-tors, while others, such as San Francisco, saw 1 As reflected in the Oppor-tunity Insights Economic Tracker data on small business revenue and small business open. For more information on data sources and methodology, see Chetty et al., 2022; “Opportunity Insights Economic Tracker,” undated.
C O R P O R A T I O N KEY FINDINGS The role that local governments play in entrepreneurial ecosystems is poorly under-stood. This exploratory report describes roles that local government actors play in the six cities that were the subject of this study. ■Those roles can be organized into four categories: convener and agenda setter, policymaker, regulation navigator, and conduit. ■City governments convene stakeholders from across the city to establish priori-ties for entrepreneurship and set an agenda for the government and other key ecosystem actors. ■Informed by insights from convenings, city governments make policies and develop programs that support entrepreneurs in either starting or scaling their business. ■City governments spend significant resources to aid entrepreneurs in navigating regulations to lower the barriers to running a small business. ■City governments serve as a conduit for federal, state, and local (nongovern-ment) funding designed to support entrepreneurship and general economic vitality. In recent years, the massive inflow of COVID-19 stimulus funds domi-nated these efforts. ■ Several local government actors undertake these roles, including mayors and their cabinet members, city council members, city administrative departments or offices, and city task forces and working groups.
Research Report 2 declines, suggesting the importance of local condi-tions to business creation and growth outcomes. Within this context, there is growing interest in entrepreneurial ecosystems among researchers and policymakers alike (Main Street America, 2022; Wurth, Stam, and Spigel, 2022). Although policy interest may have outpaced the development of a rig-orous underlying research base, some aspects of the entrepreneurial ecosystem concept are well defined. One such well-defined concept is the emphasis on the local context and on the entrepreneur as an “organi-zation, innovation, and community leader” who can influence that context in collaboration with other “entrepreneurial actors” (Wurth, Stam, and Spigel, p. 732). Entrepreneurial ecosystems encompass a wide variety of organizations, as well as institutions, programs, policies, and networks that encourage new business creation and support the growth of those businesses in a local area or region (Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City, 2020). Local policymakers are looking to support entrepreneurial ecosystems as a way to promote economic development and pathways to the middle class more generally, although the jury is still out on the effectiveness of that strategy for local and regional economies and the entrepreneurs themselves (Geraghty, 2021; Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City, 2020; Wurth, Stam, and Spigel, 2022; Shane, 2008; Stephan, Rauch, and Hatak, 2022). Despite extensive interest in entrepreneurial ecosystems as a concept and the local orientation of such ecosystems, research has not focused on the role of local government actors in fostering and maintain-ing these ecosystems, much less the success of such efforts. To learn more about the role that local gov-ernments play in entrepreneurial ecosystems and lay the groundwork for future assessments of their suc-cess, we conducted an exploratory study focused on what a diverse but limited set of city governments are doing in this space. Our study consisted of a review of publicly available materials and a series of inter-views with elected officials, city employees, nonprofit representatives, and academics across six cities iden-tified as having active entrepreneurial ecosystems: Charlotte, North Carolina; Chicago, Illinois; Detroit, Michigan; Long Beach, California; Pittsburgh, Penn-sylvania; and Seattle, Washington. Although we do not claim to generate an exhaustive list of activities undertaken by local governments nor evaluate the effectiveness of these activities in this report, our work helps lay the foundation for future research. After independently analyzing materials and interview notes, we discussed general observations about the tasks performed by government and quasi-governmental organizations active in supporting entrepreneurship in these cities. We then catego-rized tasks into four distinct but complementary roles that local governments assume. In this report, we discuss the four roles, highlighting commonali-ties and differences across cities. This exploratory, descriptive overview can serve as a starting point for a more comprehensive understanding of the role of local governments in supporting entrepreneurial ecosystems. Entrepreneurial Ecosystems and the Role of Local Government Entrepreneurial ecosystems have become a frame-work embraced by economic development practitio-ners and policymakers to strengthen local economies despite the fact that the local government role in and effect on such ecosystems is not well understood. Moore (1993, p. 76) coined the term business ecosys-tem to describe an environment in which firms “work cooperatively and competitively to support new prod-ucts, satisfy customer needs, and eventually incorpo-rate the next round of innovations.” The term “entre-preneurial ecosystem” emerged in the 2000s and has become more dominant since 2016 (Malecki, 2018). However, there is no universally accepted definition, and the concept remains underdeveloped (Stam and Spigel, 2018). Mason and Brown (2014, p. 5) defined Abbreviations COVID-19 coronavirus disease 2019 BAC Business Advisory Committee MWBE minority or women-owned business enterprise CARES Act Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act 3 an entrepreneurial ecosystem as “a set of intercon-nected entrepreneurial actors, entrepreneurial orga-nizations . . . institutions . . . and . . . processes . . . which formally and informally coalesce to connect, mediate, and govern the performance within the local entrepreneurial environment.” Entrepreneurial ecosystems have been conceptualized and analyzed at various scales and in a variety of different configura-tions. Initial conceptualizations tended to focus on high-growth and high-tech entrepreneurship, but recently, broader conceptualizations that include all businesses and organizations related to starting and owning a business have emerged (Wurth, Stam, and Spigel, 2022). The entrepreneurial ecosystem concept is closely related to concepts such as clusters, indus-trial districts, and innovation systems but focuses on the environment external to the firm rather than the behaviors of firms. The ecosystem framework emphasizes connectivity between actors and influ-ences external to the firm. A feature consistent among definitions of entrepreneurial ecosystems is the inclusion of many actors and institutions that interact to support the flow of knowledge, informa-tion, and resources in a way that encourages produc-tivity and innovation. One of the key conversations in ecosystem litera-ture concerns who the main actors are and what their roles are in facilitating business creation and growth. Several scholars have put forth models of the key components of entrepreneurial ecosystems, including key actors, institutions, and other necessary elements for a strong ecosystem (see Feld, 2012; Isenberg, 2011). One of the earliest and most-cited ecosystem models was Isenberg’s Domains of the Entrepreneur-ship Ecosystem (2011), which incorporated several elements (networks, labor, educational institutions, policy, finance, culture, support, etc.) organized into six domains. Literature on the entrepreneurial ecosystem emphasizes government as an important element based on its general support for economic develop-ment (Feld, 2012; Minniti, 2008) and, more specifi-cally, its policies around taxes, incentives, subsidies, grants, and financing with the potential to drive entrepreneurship (Pinkernell et al., 2013; Siegel, Westhead, and Wright, 2003; Stam, 2015). But much less work has focused on identifying essential or even typical roles for government and what levels of gov-ernment should be undertaking each specific policy intervention. A recent paper by Johnson et al. (2022) begins to address that gap by outlining four roles for the federal government in entrepreneurial ecosys-tems: catalyst, coordinator, certifier, and customer. However, there is some agreement that, while it may allow a role for the federal government, entrepreneur-ship is largely a local phenomenon (Audretsch and Feldman, 2004; Dahl and Sorenson, 2009; Feldman, Francis, and Bercovitz, 2005; Feldman, 2014; Gertler, 2003; Welter et al., 2016). According to Isenberg (2011, p. 11), “if we need to make a measurable impact in a reasonable time frame, and arguably at all, the greatest effort has to be concentrated in a small foot-print region, such as a city or even neighborhood.” At the same time, the capacity of local governments to devote time, attention, and resources to support-ing entrepreneurship may be limited (Gargan, 1981; Stone, 1993). The academic literature on entrepreneurial eco-systems summarized above is heavily theoretical or descriptive in nature, with little to say about the spe-cific role of local governments in the entrepreneur-ship ecosystem. There is much theorizing about the role that local government could play in ecosystems One of the key conversations in ecosystem literature concerns who the main actors are and what their roles are in facilitating business creation and growth.
4 and less in the academic research literature about what governments are doing or (especially) what they should do based on evidence of success. One strand of the literature on local entrepreneurial ecosystems, consisting of policy reports from nonprofits, trade, and advocacy organizations, offers prescriptive rec-ommendations about what local governments should be doing. Often, these recommendations are not grounded in research, but progress is being made in this area. A recent paper by Motoyama (2020) based on observations of the informal ways that mayors promote entrepreneurship identified four categories of functions performed by mayors: “(1) setting the priorities, (2) offering a clearinghouse, (3) connecting and coordinating people, and (4) appointing key per-sonnel” (p. 157). Still, there remains a need for both exploratory and confirmatory research in identifying a set of best practices for local governments in foster-ing entrepreneurial ecosystems across the country. Drawing on interviews and analysis of each city’s website, we defined what the six identified cities are doing to support entrepreneurship. We organized our discussion around four categories of activities, which we identified as local government roles in the cities we studied: convener and agenda setter, policy-maker, regulation navigator, and conduit. A variety of government actors across cities assume these roles, including mayors and their cabinet members, city council members, administrators in city departments or offices (e.g., economic development), and city task forces and working groups. Although our categori-zation structure overlaps with that of Johnson et al. (2022) and Motoyama (2020), it is differentiated from Johnson et al. by its focus on local government actors and from Motoyama by the broader range of local government and nonprofit actors we considered in our analysis. Below, we define these roles in more detail and explore variations across our sample cities. Additionally, Table 1 provides concrete examples of how these roles manifest in action.
Convener and Agenda Setter A vibrant entrepreneurial ecosystem involves dispa-rate stakeholders, including various levels and sectors of government, the private sector, local universi-ties, nonprofit organizations, citizen groups, and, of course, entrepreneurs. A primary role that we identi-fied as one played by city governments is to convene representatives from these groups to provide input on TABLE 1 Example of Roles from Studied Cities Role Examples Observed in Cities Convener and agenda setter • The Charlotte City Council funds and programmatically supports the Regional Chamber of Commerce.
• Detroit Means Business centers discussions with policymakers on a diverse coalition of small business owners.
• Pittsburgh’s economic development arm, the Urban Redevelopment Authority, is a coordinating body between the city and nongovernmental partners.
Policymaker • In Charlotte, the City Council’s Business Advisory Committee (BAC) embeds small business owners directly in the legislative process.
• Chicago made targeted investment in lab space infrastructure to support the growth of life sciences in the city.
• Detroit and Pittsburgh combined technical assistances and loans with favorable financing terms to support new small businesses.
Regulation navigator • During the COVID-19 pandemic, Chicago reanchored the permitting process in local neighborhood locations to improve access.
• Long Beach maintains a curated business portal (BizPort) that provides regulatory guidance for entrepreneurs at all stages of the business lifecycle.
• Seattle’s Office of Economic Development hosts a website with general information on small business support and supports targeting food businesses.
Conduit • In Detroit, district liaisons connect community business owners with government aid.
• In Pittsburgh, the Urban Redevelopment Authority channels funds from various levels of government and philanthropies to numerous programs supporting small businesses.
5 and define the local entrepreneurship agenda. City officials have visibility and influence over policies and issues relevant to entrepreneurship that allow them to bring people and organizations to the table for a variety of purposes. Depending on an entity’s interest and familiarity with entrepreneurship policy, local governments use their convening role to slightly different ends. For previously unengaged partici-pants, city representatives may convene stakehold-ers to get their input and build political support for policies focused on fostering a vibrant ecosystem; conversely, long-engaged entities are sometimes con-vened to identify potential barriers to success that are faced by small businesses. Another aspect of the con-vening role is the establishment of periodic feedback mechanisms that allow long-engaged entities to tap input from less-engaged participants on an ongoing basis. One interviewee spoke to the importance of building political support through these sessions, emphasizing the need to align different stakehold-ers on the “same page” before moving forward with a policy agenda. Similarly, other interviewees high-lighted their work reaching out to marginalized communities to build trust, inform policies meant to promote entrepreneurship and equitable growth in these areas, and encourage neighborhood residents to take advantage of the available resources. An interviewee from Detroit described one of their coalitions in the following way: The Detroit Means Business Coalition has brought people together around policy in a new way . . . with that group of business owners at the center, we have sort of a centrifu-gal force . . . around policy conversations where we engage other partners . . . that we may have been having one-off conversations with before as small business advocates . . . Now we have a coalition that’s building where we’re all talking kind of together and in a room. Interviewees from most cities mentioned a close working relationship with the local business com-munity facilitated through an organized network of influential leaders active in promoting business inter-est in the city. These groups were consulted for input on improving general operating conditions for entre-preneurs and were also relied on to relay information to otherwise hard-to-reach businesses across the city. For example, the City Council of Charlotte directly funds and programmatically supports the Charlotte Regional Business Alliance, the city’s chamber of commerce. Local officials rely on this entity to facilitate dialogue with entrepreneurs across the city to both establish stakeholder buy-in and relay information regarding important policy decisions impacting small businesses. In addition to the formal funding relationship between the City Council and the Charlotte Regional Business Alliance, inter-viewees from the city said they maintain an “open dialogue” with a collection of 30 academic, nonprofit, and government entities, known as the Resource Partner Team, that are committed to supporting Charlotte’s small businesses. The group was a unique source of pride for city representatives, who noted that the alliance had met regularly, unprompted, for over a decade. Officials in the cities we studied emphasized getting other stakeholders on the same page around entrepreneurship issues as critical to effectively sup-porting an entrepreneurial ecosystem. In each case, Officials in the cities we studied emphasized getting other stakeholders on the same page around entrepreneurship issues as critical to effectively supporting an entrepreneurial ecosystem. 6 the city took a leadership role in bringing key stake-holders to the table and collaborating with them to establish priorities around entrepreneurship. They then leveraged convening sessions to shape and prioritize tasks undertaken by the city government through the other three roles described below.
Policymaker Local governments facilitate a robust entrepreneurial ecosystem by ensuring that the local policy environ-ment is friendly to new and growing businesses, enabling them to thrive. There are both legislative and administrative aspects to the policymaker role. In this report, we define this role as any person who makes active choices that deploy local resources toward specific activities to establish, change, or remove requirements related to the establishment, operation, or growth of a business. In other words, the policymakers purposely choose to leverage their authority to support entrepreneurship. Further, four key dimensions of the policymaker role stood out: • crafting new or revising existing policies, pro-grams, and regulations that affect the business operating environment • developing targeted programs that directly assist certain types of businesses (e.g., small and micro businesses, minority- and women-owned business enterprises [MWBE]) • investing in the physical and institutional infrastructure to support and nurture busi-nesses (i.e., incubators, workspaces, support organizations) • leveraging the city’s customer power to sup-port local business development through pro-curement opportunities.
The policymaker role supports the strategic aims of creating and sustaining a business-friendly environment for new, small, and growing busi-nesses. This is achieved by scanning the local policy landscape, identifying ways to foster and barriers to fostering business establishment and growth, and crafting recommendations for legislative or admin-istrative actions in areas such as taxation, licensing, land use, zoning, health and safety, and environ-ment. The policymaker role can also involve funding initiatives that further these goals. The cities in our study offer some examples of approaches that can drive the attention of lawmakers and administrators toward issues related to entrepreneurship. In Detroit, the economic development agency identifies process improvements that would support new and growing businesses and was given the authority to negotiate such changes directly with city departments. Another approach is for the city council to establish a commit-tee or working groups focused on entrepreneurship or inclusive small business growth with a mandate to make budget recommendations, develop new legisla-tion, or recommend regulatory reforms. For example, the Charlotte City Council’s BAC (which consists of 20 members: two appointed by the mayor, six selected by the City Council, and 12 chosen by a diverse set of business advocacy groups) advises the City Council on how to improve business conditions throughout the city. Specifically, BAC specializes in advocating for policies that support small businesses and those owned by marginalized groups. BAC also provides stakeholders with a venue for formally sharing input on potential legislative action. In Chicago, the mayor appoints a senior official (a deputy mayor) from within the mayor’s office to serve as the point person for entrepreneurship or new business development. Although these approaches ensure high-level atten-tion to entrepreneurship in the short term, interview-ees pointed out that sustainability can be an issue during times of mayoral or councilmember turnover. Procurement policy is used across all cities in our study to support and promote the entrepre-neurial ecosystem. By devoting resources toward building the supplier base and then leveraging city government spending power to support small busi-nesses, procurement policy allows cities to carve out opportunities for new businesses and those owned by underrepresented minorities and women. Cities took different approaches and devoted varying levels of attention to procurement initiatives. Several cities created certification programs that educate business owners about the city’s procurement requirements and prepare them to respond to opportunities. These programs often target MWBEs. For example, Chi-cago’s Target Market Program, which is run through the city’s Department of Procurement Services and 7 codified in the city’s municipal code, awards general contracting opportunities to MWBEs, exclusively. Cities also use municipal resources to fund programs designed to directly support entrepreneur-ship and new businesses through loans, grants, or operational supports. In some cases, cities develop programs that can attract private resources to supple-ment the city’s investments. In this way, cities lever-age their connections to garner additional resources for new and growing businesses. Loan programs are a common tool employed by local governments to support entrepreneurship. The cities we studied often served as the primary lender of capital for communities that were not well-served by traditional financial institutions. According to interviewees, capital provided by city governments is often critical for entrepreneurs from disadvan-taged socioeconomic groups. For example, through its Small Business Mobilization Loan program, the city of Charlotte provides short-term financing with favorable interest rates to businesses certified as Small Business Enterprises. These funds are meant to bolster a business’ capacity to fulfill contracts put forth by the city’s procurement department. Chicago also provides direct financing capital to small businesses through its Small Business Improve-ment Fund. These funds are awarded as a grant and support permanent building improvements in tax increment financing districts throughout the city. SomerCor, a nonprofit lender, manages the Small Business Improvement Fund program for the city and supports and underwrites federal small business loan programs that provide capital to businesses that do not have access to traditional forms of financing. Unlike the other cities examined in this study, Seattle is barred from lending funds directly to busi-nesses by Washington state’s constitution. An inter-viewee from Seattle explicitly pointed to this law as a structural barrier limiting the types of policy levers available to help entrepreneurs in the city. Since municipal resources are often limited, cities also leverage their connections with lenders, investors, or philanthropists to establish lending, investment, or support for programs to serve local small businesses. For example, Detroit’s Motor City Match program combines tailored technical assis-tance provided by third-party, nonprofit partners with grants and capital investments to small busi-nesses interested in starting or expanding within the city limits. Motor City Match facilitates access to favorable financing opportunities through its lend-ing partners. Additionally, the program may provide grants of up to $100,000 to eligible businesses should financing options from lending partners or tradi-tional financial institutions not cover the entirety of project costs. In Pittsburgh, the Urban Redevelop-ment Authority offers several loan programs, includ-ing the Avenues of Hope Commercial Real Estate Program, Minority Business Recovery & Growth Loan Fund, Micro-Enterprise Loan Program, Accel-erating Business Expansion Loan Program, as well as a small contractor’s line of credit.
In addition to providing direct financial assis-tance to individual entrepreneurs or small businesses, city governments also cultivate a prosperous small business environment through targeted investments of municipal resources toward sectors or neighbor-hoods to address specific barriers to business creation and development facing strategic industries that have been identified by the city. The prioritization of cer-tain industry sectors or neighborhoods allows cities to have a targeted impact and reflects a policy choice. The city of Chicago developed a sector-focused post– COVID-19 economic recovery plan, which identified impediments to attracting businesses in high-growth industries. For instance, the report identified a lack of lab space to support the life sciences sector. In Procurement policy allows cities to carve out opportunities for new businesses and those owned by underrepresented minorities and women.
8 response, the city invested heavily in physical infra-structure to support the burgeoning life sciences industry in the city. This included purchasing 5 mil-lion square feet of underutilized land and providing incentives for local businesses to redevelop these properties as state-of-the-art lab space. Pittsburgh’s Avenues of Hope program is an example of how cities focus business development support for particular neighborhoods. Pittsburgh invested in redeveloping the business districts of seven historically Black neighborhoods. The invest-ment was across several dimensions, including small contractor lines of credit, real estate redevelopment loans, and grants to facilitate storefront façade improvements. All sources of funding are targeted at those who live and work in the communities and are meant to bring prosperity to Black neighborhoods without displacing longtime residents.
As illustrated in the examples above, cities lever-aged their policymaking role to support entrepre-neurial ecosystems in a variety of ways. One policy-making approach used to support entrepreneurial ecosystems is to focus explicitly and systematically on entrepreneurship and the environment for new business creation and growth generally. Another is to focus on specific sectors of the economy, includ-ing new business development in those sectors. Still another is through efforts to support specific (often historically underserved) neighborhoods or commu-nities within the city to include business development in those neighborhoods.
In the cities that we studied, officials leveraged both legislative and administrative policymak-ing responsibilities to both directly and indirectly strengthen and support entrepreneurial ecosystems. That is not to say that they always prioritize entre-preneurship over other issues in the course of poli-cymaking. Through their other entrepreneurship-related roles, local governments tackle some of the implications of local policies for entrepreneurs. Regulation Navigator One often-repeated frustration for entrepreneurs is wading through the bureaucratic red tape associated with government processes and procedures required to start and grow a business, particularly those at the local level affecting the day-to-day operation of busi-nesses. In the business navigator role, local govern-ments support the local entrepreneurial ecosystem by simplifying the underlying processes, locating resources and information in a virtual space that is convenient for entrepreneurs to access, and enhanc-ing navigation supports. These efforts aim to make it easier and more efficient for people to start and grow businesses in the city and increase the likelihood that an inclusive set of entrepreneurs will take advantage of the resources available. Interviewees emphasized their agencies’ role in helping small businesses navigate local rules, regu-lations, and requirements that could potentially discourage would-be entrepreneurs from starting a business in the city or hinder progress for established businesses. At the most basic level, all cities in our study had a centralized webpage for business owners or those looking to start a busine

enriched_points.expanded_content: Stakeholders should prioritize policies that seed entrepreneurial initiatives, create opportunities, and provide comprehensive support, ensuring alignment with local ecosystem needs and goals.
Implementing targeted investments in infrastructure, mentorship programs, and access to financing can significantly boost entrepreneurial activities and foster sustainable growth.
Recognizing the unique characteristics of local ecosystems allows for customized strategies that address specific challenges faced by entrepreneurs, enhancing overall effectiveness.
Engaging government, private sector, and educational institutions fosters a collaborative environment, enabling resource sharing, knowledge exchange, and a more robust entrepreneurial ecosystem.
